text: Colorectal Surgery Unit of Hospital Universitario Marquedo Aldesilla presents The three lymphadenectomy and subtotal colectomy in right colon cancer recurrence six years after the primary surgery. Recurrence is mainly found the first five years after colorectal surgery. Moreover, in the right colon cancer surgery, the procedure is still not well standardized, with many surgeons performing mesocolonic excision at different levels.

In this video, we will present the case of a eighty-two year old man operated six years ago due to obstructive right column cancer. It was performed a laparoscopic right amicolectomy with a standard lymphadenectomy with adult human therapy. Routine studies, six years later, show a tumor relapse in the anastomosis area, as well as speculated lymph node.
Our main goals are demonstrating the non-harvested lymph node area after this very frequently performed procedure and explain the completion of a DIII lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon localizes the previous ileocolic anastomosis and sections the terminal ileum using a linear stapler. Ileocolic vessels are identified at the site of previous ligation, and dissection is continued proximally towards their origin using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques to reach the roots of the ileocolic vein and artery.

The surgeon exposes the confluence of the ileocolic vein and the superior mesenteric vein, preparing to ligate and cut the vein for a DII lymphadenectomy. The ileocolic vein is sectioned between two locking ligation systems, while the artery, crossing behind the superior mesenteric vein, is also cut at this point.

The surgeon begins the supracolic phase by retracting the omentum to expose the gastroepiploic vein. The laser sac is opened to aid in colic mobilization, revealing tissue areas corresponding to the DII lymphadenectomy from the previous surgery.

The surgeon continues the DIII lymphadenectomy by dissecting upwards along the superior mesenteric vein until reaching the gastrocolic trunk of Henle. The colic branch of Henle is identified, ligated, and sectioned. The middle colic vessels are exposed, and both the middle colic vein and artery are sectioned, clearly visualizing the anatomy of the gastrocolic trunk of Henle.

The surgical field shows the completed DIII lymphadenectomy, revealing the vascular bed with the superior mesenteric vein and its branches, including the gastrocolic trunk of Henle and middle colic vessels.

The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic ligament up to the splenic angle, freeing it. The inferior mesenteric vein is visualized, and the left colic vein is sectioned. The inferior mesenteric artery is identified, and the left colic branch is sectioned. The descending colon is transected using linear staples. An intracorporeal anastomosis is performed between the terminal ileum and descending colon.
The surgeon manually closes both enterotomies, ensuring the openings in the intestinal walls are securely sutured.

We have been listening in the last years more often the concept of complete mesocoling excision, which is very close to the concept of DIII lymphadenectomy. Even though there is not enough evidence so as to recommend this procedure to all patients, The DII standard procedure many times is such a not standard at all, leaving variable amount of lymphoid tissue around the islecalic vessels like we just saw in the video, with uncertain results in the patient follow-up.
